Your First RAG Pipeline.nontech: Retrieval-augmented generation — or RAG — is a way of making an AI assistant answer questions using your own documents rather than just its general training. Think of it like giving the AI a filing cabinet to look through before it speaks. This talk walks through building one of those systems live, from scratch, using a standard website content system as the source material. You will see how the AI finds relevant information and where it starts making things up when it cannot. The session is labeled beginner, but that means beginner for developers — the presenter assumes you can build websites and write code. If you cannot, you will enjoy the concept but lose the thread once the live coding begins. Worth attending for the big-picture ideas; just do not expect to follow every step.
Your First RAG Pipeline.expert: This is a beginner session covering the foundational RAG loop: document chunking, embedding generation, vector similarity search, and prompt composition with retrieved context. The live build targets a CMS-backed corpus, which means the demo stack is deliberately simple and the failure modes discussed (hallucinations from poor retrieval recall, chunking artefacts) will be familiar territory if you have shipped anything in this space. There is no treatment of reranking, hybrid search, eval frameworks, or production concerns like index freshness or latency. Skip it unless you are mentoring someone new to RAG, want to borrow the teaching structure, or simply enjoy a well-paced intro delivered lakeside.

Prompt Injection: Attacking Your Own Cha.nontech: Picture a chatbot on a website that reads comments left by users — and imagine a bad actor sneaking hidden instructions into those comments to make the chatbot do things it shouldn't. That's prompt injection, and this session is a live demonstration of how those attacks actually work, along with an honest look at which defences hold up and which ones just look good on paper. The speaker assumes you already understand how large language models call external tools and how retrieval-augmented generation works — these are deep technical concepts used daily by AI engineers. This one is firmly for expert builders. If you don't already know what a vector index is, you'll be lost within the first few minutes, so this session isn't the right fit for you today.
Prompt Injection: Attacking Your Own Cha.expert: This 45-minute session is squarely at home in your schedule if you are shipping or securing LLM-backed products in production. The speaker works through four concrete attack classes against a CMS-integrated assistant: indirect prompt injection via comment fields, markdown-based data exfiltration, tool-call hijacking in agentic pipelines, and retrieval poisoning of vector indexes. Each attack gets an honest mitigation assessment — what actually holds and what is performative. Assumed baseline is solid working knowledge of RAG architectures and LLM tool-calling; no recap is offered. If you are already thinking about trust boundaries between user-generated content and your retrieval layer, this one is worth the hour.

Embeddings Are Just Coordinates.nontech: If you've ever wondered how a search engine knows that "affordable lodging" and "cheap places to stay" mean the same thing, this talk answers that question in plain English. The speaker uses maps as a running analogy to explain how AI systems turn words and ideas into coordinates — so that things with similar meanings end up sitting close together, like neighboring towns. No math required, no coding assumed. It's a genuine beginner session, and for once that label really does mean everyone. You'll walk away able to explain the concept to a colleague or a skeptical relative. Thirty minutes well spent if you work alongside AI features and want to finally understand what's actually going on under the hood.
Embeddings Are Just Coordinates.expert: This is an introductory session covering the conceptual foundations of embeddings and vector search — the map metaphor, semantic similarity as geometric proximity, and what a vector database does under the hood. None of that will be new to you. Skip it unless you are mentoring someone who needs a gentle on-ramp to the topic, you want to borrow its visualisation approach for your own teaching, or you genuinely enjoy watching a well-constructed beginner talk. Thirty minutes is a low cost, but there are almost certainly better uses of your schedule at the camp if you are already shipping RAG pipelines or tuning similarity metrics in production.

Fine-tune or RAG? Choosing Wisely.nontech: This session is about a choice that shapes almost every AI-powered product: do you bake your company's information into the AI model itself, or do you have the model look up your information on the fly each time someone asks a question? The speaker has built real products in publishing and e-commerce using both approaches and will walk through the trade-offs — cost, how up-to-date the answers are, and what happens when things go wrong. The framework sounds genuinely useful for anyone involved in scoping or approving AI work. That said, this is pitched at an intermediate technical level, so the deeper mechanics will likely blur for non-coders. You'll leave with useful vocabulary and good questions to ask your team, but this one belongs on the schedule of a technical colleague more than yours.
Fine-tune or RAG? Choosing Wisely.expert: Fine-tuning versus RAG is well-trodden territory, so here is what to calibrate against: if you already understand why RAG dominates for knowledge-freshness and auditability, parts of this will feel familiar. The value is in the concrete tradeoffs drawn from shipped publishing and e-commerce systems — cost curves, staleness failure modes, and the maintenance burden each approach accumulates over time. The decision framework may sharpen arguments you already make informally, and the war stories carry the real signal. Thirty minutes is tight, so depth is limited. Attend if you want a structured, defensible framework for stakeholder conversations; skip if you are already running both approaches in production and have the postmortems to prove it.

Agents That Edit Your CMS.nontech: This session is about teaching an AI assistant to make changes to your website's content management system — the behind-the-scenes tool editors use to organise pages, articles, and media — just by being given plain instructions in plain English. Think of it like telling a capable intern to rearrange the filing system while you make coffee. The speaker will demo this live, then explain how guardrails stop the AI from accidentally breaking your live site, how you can check what it actually did, and where it still gets things embarrassingly wrong. This is an intermediate-level talk aimed at people who already work with CMS platforms hands-on. If you do not, the live demo will be fun to watch, but the technical detail will likely lose you before the halfway mark.
Agents That Edit Your CMS.expert: This session sits at the intersection of LLM tool-calling and CMS architecture — specifically how an agent reasons over a tool registry (create content type, add field, modify taxonomy) given a natural-language instruction, and how you constrain that via permission scoping and audit logging rather than hoping the model behaves. If you have already wired function-calling or tool-use APIs into any production system, the orchestration concepts will feel familiar. The genuine value is in the failure-mode analysis — confidently wrong structured edits are a specific and underexplored hazard — and the honest capability audit at the end. Worth attending if you are evaluating agentic workflows for content infrastructure; skip if you have already shipped something like this.

Evaluating LLM Output at Scale.nontech: This session is about how to tell whether your AI-powered product is actually working well — and whether it quietly got worse after your team made a change. The speaker walks through the unglamorous plumbing behind that question: building test suites, using one AI to judge another (yes, that's a real thing, and yes, it has problems), and reading the numbers carefully enough to know signal from coincidence. Real results from real products are promised, including the awkward ones. That said, this is an expert-level talk aimed squarely at people who have already shipped AI features and write the code behind them. If that's not you, the big ideas are interesting but much of the detail will drift past you fast. Skip it and find a spot by the lake.
Evaluating LLM Output at Scale.expert: Targeted at engineers who have already pushed generative features to production and are past the "does it work at all" phase. The talk covers golden dataset maintenance under content and distribution drift, LLM-as-judge architectures with an honest accounting of their position and verbosity biases, model-version regression detection pipelines, and the statistical framing needed to distinguish genuine quality delta from evaluation noise — including effect size and sample-size considerations. Real eval suite numbers are promised, including failures. If you are wrestling with any of these in production right now, the 30-minute density is worth the slot. If you have not yet shipped a generative feature, come back to this one later.

AI on a Budget: Small Models, Big Wins.nontech: This session is about getting real work done with smaller, less expensive AI tools — think of it like choosing a reliable local restaurant over a Michelin-starred one when you just want a decent lunch. The speaker looks at tasks like sorting content, writing summaries, and flagging inappropriate material, and shows where cheaper AI holds up just fine versus where you genuinely need the pricier option. There are real cost comparisons, which could be useful if you ever approve a software budget or question a vendor's pricing. That said, this is an intermediate talk aimed at people who already build with AI — the benchmarks and code patterns will get technical fast. Go for the big-picture thinking; expect some fog when the details arrive.
AI on a Budget: Small Models, Big Wins.expert: This talk covers small open-weight model selection and deployment for common NLP workloads — classification, tagging, summarisation, extraction, moderation — benchmarked against frontier models on real CMS data. If you already know that GPT-4 is overkill for intent classification, you likely know the broad argument. The value here is in the concrete benchmark numbers and the routing architecture: a tiered dispatch pattern that gates requests by estimated complexity before deciding which model tier handles them. That cost-curve data and the specific quality-cliff findings are worth having for your next infra conversation. Attend if you are actively making or defending model-selection decisions; skip if you have already built and tuned a routing layer in production.

Hands-on: Build a Site Chatbot.nontech: In this half-day workshop you build a real chatbot — one that answers questions by reading your own website content, not just making things up. The instructors walk every camper through each step together, so you are never stuck waiting while everyone else moves on. That said, the session assumes you have at least dabbled in building or editing websites; if you have never touched a CMS (a content management system, the software behind most blogs and company sites) you may find some moments tricky. No coding beyond the basics, no maths, and no AI background is needed. A ready-to-go codebase is provided so you spend time learning, not troubleshooting. If you are comfortable around websites and curious how AI assistants actually work, this is a good use of a morning.
Hands-on: Build a Site Chatbot.expert: This half-day workshop walks through a complete RAG pipeline on a CMS: model provider integration, content indexing into a vector store, retrieval wiring, and prompt-layer guardrails. If you have already shipped an embeddings-backed search or a retrieval-augmented chatbot, none of the architecture here will surprise you. The prepared codebase and pre-issued cloud credentials signal that the pace is deliberately slow and hand-held. This is an introductory session; skip it unless you want to support the speaker, accompany a junior colleague, or steal its teaching approach for onboarding your own team to RAG fundamentals.

Zero-Downtime Deploys for Mortals.nontech: Deploying software means pushing new code to a live website — and doing it badly means your site goes dark while things update, often at the worst possible moment. This talk is about making that process so smooth and routine that nobody notices it happened. The speaker covers practical techniques: checking that a site is genuinely healthy before and after a release, updating databases without breaking things mid-swap, and having a quick undo button ready when something still goes wrong. It is billed as beginner-level, but beginner here means beginner developer — the examples are hands-on code. You will enjoy the storytelling and pick up the concepts, but the technical detail may blur quickly. Come for the ideas; sit near a friendly engineer to fill in the gaps.
Zero-Downtime Deploys for Mortals.expert: This 45-minute beginner talk covers the fundamentals of safe, zero-downtime deployment for database-backed web apps: health checks, backward-compatible schema migrations, feature flags for decoupling releases, and practiced rollback procedures, all demonstrated on a plain LAMP-style stack. If you are already running blue-green or canary deployments, working with a migrations framework like Flyway or Liquibase, and have rehearsed rollbacks in staging, nothing here will surprise you. This is an introductory session; skip it unless you want to support the speaker, accompany a junior colleague, or borrow its step-by-step teaching structure for your own internal onboarding material.

Kubernetes Without Tears.nontech: Kubernetes (usually shortened to "K8s") is a system for managing lots of software running across many computers at once — think of it as a very sophisticated traffic controller for your apps. This talk gives web teams an honest look at whether it's worth the trouble: what you actually gain, like apps that restart themselves when they break and easier scaling, versus what it costs, like complexity, quirky configuration files, and late-night headaches. It also covers simpler alternatives for teams not ready to go all-in. This is an intermediate-level DevOps session, so the details will get technical fast. As a non-technical camper, you'll pick up the big picture, but expect to lose the thread once specifics kick in. Worth attending if you're involved in decisions about your team's technical infrastructure — skip it if you're not.
Kubernetes Without Tears.expert: The talk is pitched at teams weighing Kubernetes adoption rather than those already running production clusters, so calibrate your expectations accordingly. If you are already fluent in control-plane internals, CNI plugins, or day-two operations, most of the ground covered — self-healing via liveness and readiness probes, declarative resource manifests, HPA-based scaling — will be familiar. The genuinely useful corners for experienced engineers are the comparative section on managed distros (GKE Autopilot, Fargate, Fly.io-style abstractions) and the frank treatment of lighter orchestrators like Nomad or Compose-on-Swarm as credible alternatives. Attend if you are advising a team on platform choices or want a structured framework to reuse; skip if you are already past the adoption decision.

Observability: Logs, Metrics, Traces.nontech: This session is about what happens inside a running piece of software when something goes wrong — and how engineers figure out where the problem is hiding. Think of it like the black-box recorder on a plane: logs are the written event log, metrics are the flight instruments showing speed and altitude, and traces are the moment-by-moment timeline of exactly where time was spent. The speaker will walk through a real web app example, tracking down why a checkout is slow. It is a solid talk if you work alongside developers or engineers and want to understand the tools they reach for in a crisis. That said, this is pitched at intermediate developers, so the live coding and technical detail will likely lose you fairly quickly — worth attending if you want the big picture, but go in knowing it is not aimed at you.
Observability: Logs, Metrics, Traces.expert: This 30-minute talk walks through the canonical observability triad — structured logs, time-series metrics, and distributed traces — with live instrumentation of a web app using OpenTelemetry or a comparable open standard. If you already run Prometheus, ship structured JSON logs, and have trace context propagation wired up in production, the first two thirds will be familiar ground. The potentially useful bit is the worked debugging scenario: a slow checkout flow diagnosed end-to-end, which may surface correlation patterns or tooling shortcuts worth stealing. Vendor-neutral framing keeps it honest. Attend if your tracing story is still immature or you want a clean mental model to hand to mid-level engineers; skip if your observability stack is already coherent.

Postmortems That Don't Assign Blame.nontech: When something goes wrong at work and customers notice, what happens in the meeting afterward matters more than most people realise. This talk is about running a postmortem, which is simply the conversation a team has to understand what broke and why. The big idea is doing that without pointing fingers at individuals, and instead asking why the system made a mistake easy to happen. The speaker walks through a real incident review, lightly disguised, with some humour alongside the practical lessons. As a non-technical camper, you won't need to follow any code here. The ideas are about people, process, and honest communication. Worth going to.
Postmortems That Don't Assign Blame.expert: This 30-minute talk covers the fundamentals of blameless postmortem culture: timeline reconstruction, systems-thinking framing over individual attribution, actionable remediation items, and managing organizational pressure to assign fault. The fictionalised incident walkthrough is a nice touch for teaching purposes. You almost certainly know this material — psychological safety in incident review is well-trodden ground for anyone who has run SRE or on-call rotations. Skip it unless you are mentoring someone new to incident culture, want to borrow the walkthrough format for your own team training, or are curious how the speaker handles the 'manager wants a name' dynamic, which is occasionally where the real craft lives.

Chaos Engineering on a Friday.nontech: Chaos engineering is the practice of deliberately breaking things in a controlled way — killing processes, filling up storage, cutting off connections — before a real failure does it for you at the worst possible moment. The idea is to test how a system actually behaves under stress, rather than guessing. This talk walks through how one team runs those experiments safely with real clients, including how they stop a test from becoming an actual outage, and what needs to be in place inside a team before you start unplugging things on purpose. Because this session is aimed at expert engineers with deep production experience, it will get technical fast. Come for the stories; skip it if you want to understand the concepts from scratch.
Chaos Engineering on a Friday.expert: Thirty minutes of practitioner-level chaos engineering covering fault injection across the four canonical failure axes: process death, disk saturation, network partition, and dependency misbehaviour. The speaker walks through a structured game-day format with explicit blast-radius controls — the kind of guardrails that separate a controlled experiment from a P1. There is also coverage of the organisational prerequisites: blameless culture, runbook readiness, and stakeholder buy-in, which are often the real blockers in production environments. Real experiment results are included, failures and all. If you already run chaos experiments you will be comparing notes; if you have been meaning to start, this gives you a reproducible framework. Attend.

GitOps: Your Cluster as a Pull Request.nontech: GitOps is an approach to managing computer infrastructure — the servers and systems that keep software running — where every change is recorded and approved through a shared document system (called a repository, think of it as a very structured shared folder) rather than someone typing commands directly into a machine and hoping they remember what they did. This talk shows that idea in action on a live system, including a deliberate break-it-and-watch-it-fix-itself demo. The concepts are explainable, and you will likely follow the story even without a technical background. That said, this is aimed at people who already work with infrastructure day-to-day, so the details will get technical fast. Worth attending if you want to understand why developers get excited about this approach — but go in knowing the second half may lose you.
GitOps: Your Cluster as a Pull Request.expert: The session covers GitOps fundamentals — repo as source of truth, operator-driven reconciliation, PR-based change flow — territory most senior engineers will already know if they've touched Flux or Argo CD in production. The live demo (merge → reconcile → verify → deliberate sabotage → self-heal) is well-trodden but can be useful as a reference mental model. The parts worth your time are the awkward corners: secrets management, out-of-band emergency changes that bypass the GitOps loop, and what a revert actually means once a schema migration has already applied. If those edge cases are still fuzzy in your current setup, the 30 minutes are worth it. If you've already fought those battles, skip it.

The Art of the Rollback.nontech: Deploying software means sending updates to a live product — and sometimes those updates go wrong. This talk is about having a solid plan to reverse a bad update quickly, instead of scrambling to fix it under pressure. Think of it like rehearsing a fire drill: boring until the day you actually need it. The speaker argues that knowing how to undo a release is just as important as knowing how to do one. It is a beginner-level talk aimed at developers, so some of the technical detail — particularly around database migrations — will drift out of reach. That said, the core idea is clear and genuinely useful for anyone involved in shipping products. Worth attending if you are curious about how software teams manage risk.
The Art of the Rollback.expert: This 15-minute talk covers rollback as a deliberate pipeline primitive rather than a panic response: roll-forward culture and its risk surface, database migration patterns that preserve reversibility (expand/contract being the obvious candidate), and the argument for rehearsed rollbacks over ad-hoc recovery. If you already run blue/green or canary deployments with automated rollback gates and have a disciplined schema migration strategy, most of this will be familiar ground. The one novel hook is the team's specific one-line change, which may or may not be worth 15 minutes of your afternoon. This is a beginner session; skip it unless you're mentoring junior engineers, want to borrow its framing for onboarding material, or are genuinely curious about that one change.

Scaling Databases Past the Comfort Zone.nontech: This session digs into what happens when a database — think of it as the giant filing cabinet powering an app — starts buckling under serious traffic. The speaker covers advanced techniques like splitting data across multiple servers, managing thousands of simultaneous connections, and making structural changes to a live system without taking it offline. These are real war stories from production systems, not toy examples. That said, this is firmly an expert-level talk aimed at engineers who already operate databases day to day. If you don't write code or manage infrastructure, the specifics will be hard to follow from the first few minutes. Skip this one and find a session that fits you better — there are plenty at the camp.
Scaling Databases Past the Comfort Zone.expert: This 45-minute expert session covers the operational failure modes that appear once a database is genuinely load-bearing: read-replica replication lag and consistency violations, connection pool exhaustion and its cascading effects, partition-key selection pitfalls and the query plans they silently invalidate, zero-downtime schema migrations at billion-row scale, and the organisational friction that precedes any sharding decision. Each pattern is grounded in real incident timelines rather than theory. If you have wrestled with pt-online-schema-change versus gh-ost trade-offs, or watched a range partition turn a fast query slow, there may still be useful specifics here. The Q&A is framed as open consulting. Attend if you are actively running large relational workloads; the incident-backed framing makes it worth the seat.

CSS Has Grown Up: Grid, Container Querie.nontech: CSS — the language that controls how websites look — has quietly had a glow-up, and this session shows off what it can do now. The speaker walks through a handful of new tools: one lets a design block reshape itself based on its surroundings rather than just the screen size, another finally lets you style a parent element based on what's inside it, and a third ends the long-running war over which style rules take priority. Everything is demonstrated live on real code, so if code on a screen makes your eyes glaze over, parts of this will feel abstract. The ideas themselves, though, are worth catching — they explain why modern websites behave so much more fluidly than they used to. Honest verdict: this is aimed at people who already write CSS day to day. Come for the concepts, but expect the live-coding stretches to lose you.
CSS Has Grown Up: Grid, Container Querie.expert: Modern CSS has moved far past the layout hacks you probably still carry as muscle memory. This 45-minute live-coding session covers container queries for true context-aware component responsiveness, the :has() relational pseudo-class for parent and sibling selection logic, subgrid for cross-nested alignment without wrapper gymnastics, and cascade layers as a principled replacement for specificity triage. If you work regularly with a component library, you likely know grid and custom properties cold — the value here is in the corners: how :has() interacts with specificity, where subgrid still breaks down, and which JS abstractions become genuinely redundant. Worth attending if you haven't stress-tested these features in production; skip if you already have.

Accessibility Is Not a Checklist.nontech: Most websites claim to be accessible but quietly fail the people who need them most. This session shows you what that failure feels like — the speaker runs a real website using only a keyboard and a screen reader (software that reads pages aloud for people with visual impairments) so you can see, and hear, exactly where things break. Then it walks through the habits that fix those problems before they start. No jargon, no coding required to follow along. This is a beginner-level talk, and the big ideas land cleanly even without a technical background. If you work on websites, commission them, or write content for them, this one is worth your 45 minutes.
Accessibility Is Not a Checklist.expert:
This is a beginner session covering foundational accessibility habits: semantic HTML, keyboard focus management, and integrating screen-reader testing into a regular workflow. Live demos with a screen reader and keyboard-only navigation anchor the talk. If you already know why `
` is an anti-pattern, understand ARIA landmark roles, and have wired focus traps in modals, there is little technical novelty here. Skip it unless you are mentoring someone newer to frontend work, looking for a structured way to frame accessibility culture conversations with your team, or want to audit how someone else teaches these fundamentals. Otherwise, your 45 minutes are better spent elsewhere at camp.

Design Tokens for Multi-brand Sites.nontech: Design tokens are a way of storing all the visual decisions for a website — colours, fonts, spacing — as named building blocks that designers and developers share. This talk is for people who already work in that space: it gets into the specifics of structuring those building blocks when one codebase has to serve multiple brands at once, and how a designer's change in a design tool called Figma can flow through to a code review without anyone losing their mind. If you are not already involved in design systems or front-end development, the broad ideas are interesting but the detail will move fast. Comfortable with design systems? This half-hour is practical and battle-tested — worth your time.
Design Tokens for Multi-brand Sites.expert: The core token taxonomy — global, alias, and component-scoped layers — will likely be familiar territory, but the specifics here are worth your attention: the talk focuses on semantic naming conventions tied to brand decisions rather than CSS primitives, which is where most token systems quietly rot over time. The Figma-to-PR pipeline and dark mode as a structural theme rather than a post-hoc inversion are the parts most likely to surface something you haven't fully solved in production. Drawn from three years and two rewrites on a real multi-brand codebase, so the failure modes should be concrete. Thirty minutes is short; attend if your current token architecture is showing seams across brands or themes, skip if yours is already airtight.

Web Components in the Real World.nontech: This session is a deep technical dive into the nuts and bolts of building and maintaining a component library using a browser technology called Web Components — a way of creating reusable interface pieces that work across different software frameworks without being tied to any one of them. The speaker has shipped this stuff in the real world and is here to share the hard lessons. Terms like shadow DOM, server-side rendering, and ElementInternals will come up often and without much explanation. You will enjoy the storytelling and pick up a sense of why design systems are genuinely complicated, but the detail will move fast. This one is aimed squarely at expert frontend developers. If that is not you, feel free to skip it and save your energy for a better-fit session.
Web Components in the Real World.expert: Thirty minutes of hard-won production knowledge on web components deployed across a multi-property, multi-framework design system. The speaker covers shadow DOM encapsulation trade-offs and intentional theming API design, declarative shadow DOM for SSR, ElementInternals for native form participation, event contract patterns that don't break under React or Vue wrappers, and accessibility edge cases that rarely surface in documentation. If you've already wrestled with FOUC in DSD or discovered that delegatesFocus has opinions, this is pitched at your level. Good fit for anyone maintaining or architecting a serious component library at scale. Attend.

Stop Shipping JavaScript.nontech: This lightning talk argues that many websites send far more JavaScript — the programming language that makes web pages interactive — than they actually need, because browsers have quietly gained built-in ways to do the same things. The speaker will swap out common features like pop-up dialogs, collapsible sections, and smooth scrolling, replacing them with native browser tools and showing the page get leaner in real time. It is an intermediate frontend talk, meaning you will enjoy the enthusiasm and the before-and-after reveal, but the live code edits will move fast and assume you already build websites. If you do not, come for the energy and the central idea — do less, on purpose — but expect some of the detail to blur past you.
Stop Shipping JavaScript.expert: A fifteen-minute live refactor replacing JS-driven accordions, dialogs, client-side form validation, lazy loading, smooth scrolling, and view transitions with their native HTML and CSS equivalents — details/summary, dialog, required/pattern, loading="lazy", scroll-behavior, and the View Transitions API. If you already know the platform primitives cold, the live bundle-shrink demo is the main draw; the framing as a triage discipline rather than framework hatred is worth noting. Intermediate-pitched, so the individual substitutions will be familiar, but the consolidated before/after and Lighthouse delta could sharpen arguments you make to teammates still reaching for libraries by reflex. Attend if you mentor frontend engineers or want a tight reference talk; skip if you already run this playbook in production.

Dark Mode Done Right.nontech: Dark mode sounds simple — just flip the colours, right? It turns out there is quite a bit of craft involved in making it look good rather than just technically present. This short 15-minute talk walks through the common mistakes (think glowing, eye-straining text on a pure black background) and explains the design choices that separate a pleasant dark mode from a painful one: things like toning down your brand colours, handling shadows differently, and making sure the right theme appears instantly without an ugly flash. No coding knowledge is needed to follow the main ideas, though the speaker will dip into some CSS specifics. If you care about how products feel to use, this is worth your fifteen minutes.
Dark Mode Done Right.expert: This fifteen-minute lightning talk covers prefers-color-scheme, the color-scheme property, three-state toggle UX, desaturating brand colours, lifting shadows for elevation, and flash-of-wrong-theme prevention — all solid, well-documented CSS fundamentals. If you have shipped a dark mode before, you know most of this already. This is an introductory session; skip it unless you want to support the speaker, accompany a junior colleague, or steal its teaching approach for onboarding your own team.

Single Directory Components Deep Dive.nontech: This session is about a specific way of organising the building blocks of a Drupal website — think of it like a filing system where every piece of a webpage (its look, its behaviour, its structure) lives together in one tidy folder instead of scattered across a project. The speaker goes deep into the technical craft of designing and connecting those building blocks at scale. This is firmly an expert-level talk aimed at people who already build Drupal themes for a living. If you don't know what Twig or a render array is, the first few minutes will feel like a foreign language and it won't get easier. Skip this one and find a session that matches where you are — there's no shame in that at camp.
Single Directory Components Deep Dive.expert: This session digs into Single Directory Components at the architecture level — schema-as-contract with practical validation tradeoffs, the slots-vs-props API design decision and when each breaks down, cross-theme and cross-module composition without inheritance coupling, and how SDCs integrate with render arrays and layout builder under real page-builder workloads. Performance benchmarks under load are included. Solid Twig and Drupal theme-layer fluency is assumed, so if you have been burned by component API drift or theme inheritance tangles in production, this is worth your 45 minutes. Expert-level, well-matched to senior Drupal frontend engineers and architects. Attend.

Prototyping with Real Content.nontech: This talk is about a simple but easy-to-overlook design habit: using real words, real names, and real images when you are mocking up a website or app, instead of the classic filler text that starts "Lorem ipsum." The speaker argues — with some good evidence — that pretend content hides problems that only show up when actual messy data arrives. The session covers quick ways to gather realistic sample content and a clever trick for getting stakeholders involved early. No coding required at any point. This is a beginner-level design talk, and the ideas are genuinely accessible to non-technical campers. If you work with product teams, review designs, or just want to understand why shipped things sometimes look different from the mockups, this 30-minute session is worth your time.
Prototyping with Real Content.expert: This is an introductory session aimed at designers and frontend newcomers who are still reaching for lorem ipsum by default. The core argument — that realistic content stress-tests layouts earlier and reduces late-stage rework — is sound, but if you already structure your Storybook stories around production data snapshots, seed your design tokens with real copy edge cases, or have run a content audit before, there is nothing here you have not lived through. The techniques covered are quick content audits, curated sample datasets mirroring actual field-length variance, and the stakeholder-writes-the-placeholder trick. Skip it unless you are mentoring a junior, accompanying a designer new to content-first thinking, or want to borrow its framing for your own internal talks.

API-First CMS: Decouple Without Regret.nontech: This session is about a specific way of building websites where the system editors use to manage content is kept completely separate from what visitors actually see — an approach sometimes called "headless" or "decoupled" CMS. The speaker has done this both well and badly, and is sharing the real-world lessons. The ideas — like when to separate things, when not to, and how to keep editors happy — are genuinely interesting for non-technical campers to overhear. But this is an intermediate backend talk, and the practical detail (API design, caching layers, preview architecture) assumes you already build software for a living. You'll catch the shape of the argument, but expect to lose the thread once the specifics kick in around minute ten or fifteen.
API-First CMS: Decouple Without Regret.expert: The talk assumes you already know why headless CMS architectures are appealing, so the motivational case gets skipped quickly. What you probably know cold: REST or GraphQL content APIs, CDN caching basics, the general decoupled topology. Where it may still earn its 45 minutes: the specific decision framework around editorial preview fidelity, per-route caching invalidation tied to content freshness, and the practical heuristics for identifying pages that should stay coupled. The speaker brings real post-mortems, not theory, and the pre-engagement checklist suggests opinionated, hard-won criteria. Worth attending if you are actively scoping or auditing headless builds; skip it if you have already shipped several and developed your own triage process.

Queues Save Lives.nontech: When you click a button on a website and nothing seems to happen for ages, there's a good chance the server behind the scenes is trying to do ten things at once before it can reply. This talk is about teaching software to say "I'll deal with that in a moment" — handing slow or complicated jobs off to a waiting list (a queue) so the app stays fast for everyone. The speaker builds up that idea from scratch using plain, practical examples. It's listed as beginner-level, but that means beginner for developers — code will appear on screen. You'll follow the core idea easily enough, but the technical detail will get fuzzy fast if you don't write software. Worth a seat if the big-picture concept interests you; otherwise, maybe pick a different session.
Queues Save Lives.expert: This 30-minute talk covers queue fundamentals: synchronous-versus-asynchronous work decomposition, at-least-once delivery semantics, idempotency requirements, retry/backoff strategies, and dead-letter queue patterns, all demonstrated against a plain database-backed queue rather than a dedicated broker. If you already know why a job consumer must be idempotent, have wired up Sidekiq or Celery backoff policies, and have debugged a DLQ in production, there is nothing here you have not seen. This is an introductory session — skip it unless you want to support the speaker, accompany a junior colleague, or borrow its from-first-principles teaching structure for your own onboarding materials.

What's New in Modern PHP.nontech: PHP is a programming language that powers a huge chunk of the web — think WordPress, Facebook's origins, countless online shops. This talk is a quick tour of how PHP has quietly grown up over the last few years, covering new tools that help developers write cleaner, less repetitive code. The speaker walks through real examples from working software, not textbook puzzles, so you get a sense of what these changes mean in practice. That said, this is pitched at developers who already work with PHP day to day. If you don't write code, you'll catch the enthusiasm and maybe a few headline ideas, but the specifics will blur fast. Skip it unless you have a technical co-founder you're trying to understand better.
What's New in Modern PHP.expert: If your PHP knowledge is current through 8.x you will recognise most of this ground — enums, readonly classes, first-class callables and fibers are not new news. The session earns its intermediate tag rather than beginner, so the before-and-after production framing may surface idioms worth comparing against your own codebase conventions, particularly around property hooks and asymmetric visibility, which landed in 8.4 and see less coverage than the earlier features. The JIT section is honest about its limited gains on typical web workloads, which is the correct take and worth hearing if you ever field questions about it. At 30 minutes it is tight. Attend if you want a structured refresher on the 8.1–8.4 arc or are mentoring engineers still on 7.4; skip if you are already shipping idiomatic 8.4 code daily.

Designing Webhooks People Can Trust.nontech: Webhooks are a way for one piece of software to tap another on the shoulder and say "something just happened." They sound simple, but when they go wrong — say, a payment gets charged twice because a message was sent twice — things get messy fast. This talk is a deep technical session aimed squarely at software engineers who already build and run these systems day to day. The speaker walks through the unglamorous but genuinely important stuff: how to send messages safely, handle failures gracefully, and know when something went wrong. As a non-technical camper, you may enjoy the war-story framing, but the detail will get dense quickly. Probably skip this one.
Designing Webhooks People Can Trust.expert: The session targets the failure modes that bite real webhook infrastructure: HMAC signing with proper secret rotation, idempotency key design and consumer-side deduplication to survive at-least-once delivery, the tradeoffs of ordered delivery guarantees (and why dropping them often wins), retry backoff with jitter plus dead-letter and give-up strategies, replay pipelines for lagging consumers, and structured observability so delivery health is queryable rather than ticket-driven. Thirty minutes means the speaker has clearly done the curation work already. If you have built or debugged webhook delivery at any scale beyond toy integrations, there are enough specific patterns here to justify the slot. Attend.

The Repository Pattern Is Overrated.nontech: This lightning talk is a good-natured argument among experienced software builders about a common way of organising backend code. The repository pattern is a design approach where developers add an extra layer between their application and its database — the speaker thinks that layer usually causes more trouble than it solves. If those words mean little to you, that is a fair sign this one is aimed elsewhere. The talk is pitched at expert level, meaning it assumes years of hands-on software development; the whole debate rests on lived experience with codebases and testing patterns. Come for the atmosphere if you like, but expect to be lost well before the halfway point. Best saved for the engineers in your group.
The Repository Pattern Is Overrated.expert: A tight 15-minute argument against repository pattern orthodoxy in typical web stacks. The speaker targets the specific failure modes senior engineers have likely already muttered about: leaky abstractions that mirror ORM APIs method-for-method, test suites that mock the repository into vacuousness, and the persistent myth of database-portability that justifies none of the indirection cost. The constructive half covers when a naked query is the right call and the narrower contexts where the pattern still pays off. If you have shipped this pattern and defended it in code review, there is enough here to sharpen your thinking or at least your counterarguments. Attend.

Search: From LIKE to Vectors.nontech: This session traces how search works under the hood, starting from the simplest database query and climbing all the way to AI-powered search that understands meaning rather than just matching words. Think of it like going from a basic card catalogue to a librarian who actually grasps what you're looking for. The speaker uses live demos and real code throughout, which is where the intermediate rating kicks in — the concepts are explainable in plain English, but the session is built around software engineering details. You'll pick up a solid mental model of why search is hard and how modern tools solve it, but expect the technical portions to move fast. Worth attending if you're curious about the big picture; not the best fit if hands-on code examples are likely to lose you entirely.
Search: From LIKE to Vectors.expert: The session traces the full search stack from naive SQL LIKE patterns through inverted-index full-text search (relevance scoring, BM25, faceting, typo tolerance) and into embedding-based semantic retrieval and hybrid keyword-plus-vector pipelines. If you already tune Elasticsearch or Postgres full-text daily, the first third will be familiar ground. The value is likely in the semantic and hybrid sections — specifically how embedding models are wired into retrieval, where recall and precision trade off, and what five concrete search engines look like against the same dataset. Honest trade-off framing across the board is a good sign for production applicability. Worth attending if hybrid retrieval is on your near-term roadmap; skip if you are already running it in production.

Caching Layers Explained with Sandwiches.nontech: Imagine a busy sandwich shop that keeps a tray of pre-made orders near the counter so staff don't rebuild every sandwich from scratch each time. Websites work the same way — they quietly store copies of things in several different places so they don't have to go and fetch or rebuild the same information over and over. This talk walks through where all those copies live, why they sometimes show you outdated content (like an old headline that stubbornly refuses to disappear), and how the people who build sites actually fix that. The sandwich metaphor does a lot of heavy lifting. You'll follow the concepts comfortably, though the brief code moments may blur a little. Worth going — it's a genuinely useful mental model for anyone who has ever wondered why refreshing a page didn't seem to do anything.
Caching Layers Explained with Sandwiches.expert: This is an introductory session walking through browser cache, CDN, reverse proxy, render cache, and database buffers via a sandwich-shop metaphor. The invalidation coverage — cache tags in particular — is the one moment that occasionally trips up developers who learned caching piecemeal, but if you already know why `Cache-Control: stale-while-revalidate` and surrogate keys exist, there is nothing here you have not seen. The talk is 30 minutes and clearly aimed at people who still find stale-content bugs mysterious. Skip it unless you are accompanying a junior colleague, want to poach the teaching metaphor for your own onboarding material, or genuinely enjoy a well-structured sandwich analogy on a slow afternoon at camp.

Event Sourcing: A Cautionary Tale.nontech: Event sourcing is a way of building software where, instead of saving the current state of something, you save every event that ever happened to it — like keeping every bank statement instead of just your balance. This speaker tried it for their order system and lived to tell the tale. The talk covers what went wrong: histories that took hours to replay, customer data that was nearly impossible to delete under privacy law, and confusion that crept into everyday support calls. They'll share the messier, more practical system they ended up building. This is an expert-level technical session with real code and architecture decisions at its core — great for developers, but if you don't build software, you'll likely lose the thread pretty quickly. Skip this one and catch them at the campfire instead.
Event Sourcing: A Cautionary Tale.expert: A production postmortem on event-sourcing an order management system, covering the failure modes that only surface at scale: schema evolution against an immutable log, multi-hour replay times, eventual consistency bleeding into support and ops workflows, and GDPR right-to-erasure colliding head-on with an append-only store. The speaker lands on a hybrid architecture — event sourcing scoped to where the audit trail justifies the overhead, CRUD everywhere else — and offers a decision framework for drawing that line. If you have shipped or are evaluating event sourcing in anger, the specifics here are worth 45 minutes. Attend.

Your First Core Contribution.nontech: This session is about taking your very first step as someone who helps improve open-source software — programs that are built and maintained by volunteers sharing their work freely with the world. The speaker walks through the whole process live, finding a small bug, fixing it, and submitting the fix, stumbles included. If you have never heard of a "merge request" or "issue queue," you will pick up the general story, but the session assumes you can already write at least a little PHP or JavaScript code. If you don't, the big picture is still interesting, but the practical detail will slip away fast. Worth a look if you're code-curious; better skipped if you're fully non-technical and tight on time.
Your First Core Contribution.expert: This is an introductory session walking first-time contributors through the full loop on a live novice-tagged issue: dev environment setup, bug reproduction, fix, test, and merge request submission in PHP or JavaScript. The triage workflow and reviewer expectations are covered, but at an orientation level. Nothing here will be new to anyone who has already landed a patch in an open source project. Skip it unless you are mentoring a junior camper and want to observe the framing, or you are curious how the speaker structures the live-coding arc as a teaching device. As a reference for your own onboarding materials it has some potential; as technical content for you personally, it does not.

Maintaining a Module Without Burning Out.nontech: This session is for people who've built something small that quietly became someone else's dependency — and now feel the weight of that. The speaker walks through the human side of running an open-source project: how to manage the inbox without it eating your weekends, how to say no without feeling guilty, and how to know when it's time to pass the torch or simply stop. No coding knowledge is needed to follow the ideas here; the talk is really about boundaries, communication, and self-preservation. It sits at an intermediate level, meaning it assumes you've been through this experience. If you haven't maintained a public project yet, you'll still find it thought-provoking — but the practical tips will land harder once you have.
Maintaining a Module Without Burning Out.expert: This talk targets the psychological and operational overhead of open source maintenance rather than any specific toolchain. If you already run a well-triaged repo with documented contribution policies and a co-maintainer pipeline, most of the ground here — issue templates, semantic versioning discipline, CVE response runbooks — will feel familiar. The corners worth checking: the session's framing of deprecation policy as a first-class design decision, and its explicit treatment of succession versus sunset as separate choices with different community obligations. Thirty minutes is tight, so expect practical heuristics over deep dives. Attend if your maintenance load is quietly eroding your evenings; skip if your project governance is already solid.

Running a Local Meetup That Survives.nontech: If you have ever thought about starting a community group, or you help run one already, this talk is a pleasant surprise — it has nothing to do with writing code. The speaker runs a local tech meetup that has stayed alive for seven years, which is genuinely rare, and they are sharing the practical playbook: how to find venues that do not vanish when a sponsor drops out, how to keep a steady stream of speakers without leaning on the same four friends, and how to stop the whole thing depending on one exhausted person. The ideas here apply to any kind of regular community gathering, not just tech ones. Beginner label, zero technical knowledge needed — go.
Running a Local Meetup That Survives.expert: This is an introductory session covering meetup operations: venue logistics, speaker pipeline diversification, the two-organiser redundancy rule, fallback formats for low-submission months, and retention-rate as a health metric over raw headcount. None of that is technically complex — the value is in the distilled seven-year operational playbook, not novel engineering concepts. If you already run or have run a community group, most of this will confirm what you learned the hard way. Skip it unless you are spinning up a new meetup, mentoring someone who is, or want to borrow its framework as a model for onboarding future co-organizers at your own user group.

Funding Open Source: What Actually Works.nontech: Much of the software the modern world runs on is built and maintained by unpaid volunteers working in their spare time — and many of those volunteers are burning out. This session is a practical, numbers-included look at the different ways companies try to give back: sponsorships, paid contributors, staff time, and more. The speaker has tried most of them and will tell you honestly what worked, what backfired, and what the simplest answer turned out to be. No coding knowledge needed here — this is really about money, sustainability, and organisational choices. The intermediate label reflects the speaker's background, not a barrier to entry. If you influence budgets or care where your tools come from, this one is worth your time.
Funding Open Source: What Actually Works.expert: The talk walks through five funding mechanisms — sponsorships, bounties, support contracts, direct maintainer hires, and paid staff-time contributions — with actual budget figures and outcome data from one engineering leader's experience. You likely already understand the dependency risk and have opinions on bounties distorting contributor incentives; the value here is the comparative accounting across all five in a single production context, plus the speaker's conclusion about which low-complexity option outperformed the rest. Useful if you are currently making or defending a budget case for upstream investment. Skip if you have already run several of these models yourself and are past the evaluation stage.

Mentoring at Scale.nontech: This session is about building a system for welcoming newcomers into a community or team — not just pairing people up informally, but creating something repeatable that actually works at scale. The speakers have helped over a thousand first-time contributors get started, and they share what held up: how to run events where beginners do real things rather than just follow along, how to coach experienced people to guide without taking over, and how to measure whether newcomers stick around. The technical examples are aimed at software communities, but the human dynamics translate well. If you work in community building, team culture, or onboarding in any form, you'll take away practical ideas. Worth going.
Mentoring at Scale.expert: You likely know the basics of contribution funnels and have run at least one onboarding event, so the fundamentals here will not surprise you. Where this session earns its intermediate label is in the operational detail: matching strategies that account for timezone, domain overlap, and contributor bandwidth rather than just enthusiasm; mentor training focused on keyboard-off facilitation; and a retention measurement approach that distinguishes genuine second contributions from polite one-offs. The sprint-day playbook is concrete and portable. If you have already iterated your own programme through multiple cycles and track cohort retention systematically, the novelty is thin. If you are still guessing at which variables actually move the needle on repeat contribution, attend.

Docs Are a Love Language.nontech: Good documentation is the difference between a product that feels welcoming and one that makes people feel stupid. This quick fifteen-minute talk makes the case for writing docs that actually help people — and gives you a practical framework for doing it. You will learn about the four distinct types of documentation and why jumbling them together creates confusion, how to write for someone who is stressed and skimming fast, and why examples and error messages are part of the docs whether you planned it that way or not. No coding knowledge needed whatsoever — this is really about clear writing and understanding your reader. If you work on any product, internal tool, or project with a help page, you will get something useful here. Definitely go.
Docs Are a Love Language.expert: This is an introductory session covering the Diátaxis-adjacent four-quadrant documentation taxonomy (tutorials, how-tos, explanations, reference), skimmability heuristics, example-driven writing, and treating empty states and error messages as first-class docs. Fifteen minutes means breadth, not depth — you almost certainly know the four-types framework already, and the panic-reader framing won't be news. The one potentially useful angle: the single review question ('what does the reader want to do right now?') as a quick audit heuristic you could hand to a junior team member. Skip this unless you are mentoring someone new to open source contribution or want to borrow its teaching structure for your own onboarding docs.

From Issue Queue to Release Manager.nontech: Open source projects — the shared software that powers much of the internet — are run by volunteers and staff engineers who climb an unofficial career ladder: from filing bug reports, to reviewing others' work, to finally cutting the official releases that everyone downloads. This talk maps that hidden progression in detail, covering the judgment calls, coordination headaches, and trust-building that senior contributors navigate every day. The language and examples will assume deep hands-on experience with open source contribution. As a non-technical camper, you may enjoy the window into how volunteer-driven software actually gets made — but be ready to lose the thread once specifics kick in, which will be early. Worth a curious peek if the topic interests you; not the best use of your day if you want to leave with actionable takeaways.
From Issue Queue to Release Manager.expert: This talk maps the unwritten progression from high-volume issue triage to release stewardship in a real open source project. The concrete material covers signal extraction from large issue queues, review practices that transfer context rather than just block merges, semver contract enforcement under release pressure, coordinating CVE embargoes across a distributed volunteer pool, and the trust mechanics around commit-bit delegation. If you have already held committer rights and managed a release cycle you will recognize most of the territory, but the embargo coordination and cross-timezone volunteer dynamics sections are specific enough to surface friction you may not have named yet. Thirty minutes is tight; attend if you are moving toward release management or want a precise vocabulary for what that work actually costs.

Contribution Sprint Bootcamp.nontech: Open source software is built by volunteers who submit small improvements to shared projects — think of it like a community garden where anyone can plant something. This full-day workshop walks you through making your very own contribution to a real project, from setting up your computer to submitting a change and responding to feedback, all before the camp bonfire. The good news: you do not have to write code. Documentation fixes, design input, and testing all count. Mentors stay close throughout the day in small groups. The session is genuinely beginner-friendly, and non-technical campers have a real place here — bring a laptop, a little patience, and your name could be on something shipped by sundown.
Contribution Sprint Bootcamp.expert: This full-day workshop walks first-time contributors through environment setup, issue triage, patch submission, and code review response — the complete beginner arc, with mentors holding hands at each step. The concrete workflow (dev environment, issue queue navigation, writing tests, responding to review) is sound pedagogy, but nothing here will be new to anyone who has landed even a handful of merged PRs. This is an introductory session; skip it unless you want to support the speaker, accompany a junior colleague, or observe how the contribution lifecycle is taught — that last angle could be genuinely useful if you mentor early-career engineers or run your own project's contributor onboarding.
